title: Isanya Desikar
subtitle: (1750 - January 1829)
meta_description: Isanya Desikar was a distinguished yogi born in 1750 who settled at the north-eastern side of Arunachala and composed 117 advaitic hymns to the mountain, drawing devotees including a British District Collector.
body_html:
Isanya Desikar, whose math is located just outside Tiruvannamalai on the old pradakshina road, was a distinguished yogi who felt the spiritual call of Arunachala. He came and settled at the foot of the mountain quite late in his life, and by virtue of his intense and personal relationship with Arunachaleswara, is regarded as one of the major saints of Arunachala.

He was born in 1750 in a small village called Rayavelur near the River Palar in northern Tamil Nadu. His parents prayed to Lord Murugan at Arunachala for a son, and when their prayers were answered, the child was given the name Kandappan, one of the many names of Lord Murugan. From an early age he was precocious both spiritually and intellectually. His father gave him Siva diksha at the age of seven and bestowed the title 'Desikar' on him.

At sixteen, refusing marriage, he persuaded his mother to let him go on pilgrimage and set off on an extensive South India yatra. At Chidambaram he encountered Sri Mouna Swami, a local siddha purusha who lived immersed in samadhi. After demonstrating his earnestness by standing undaunted in heavy winter rain, Isanya Desikar received initiation and upadesa from Mouna Swami.

He spent many years in solitary meditation in a cave near Vettavalam, but when crowds began importuning him to manufacture gold, he quietly slipped away to Arunachala and settled on the banks of the Goraknath tank.

Shortly afterwards, invisible siddhas who resided at Arunachala escorted him to a cave on the mountain filled with ripe fruits, fulfilling a devotee's prayer for a son. As Isanya Desikar walked toward a rendezvous with his old supporter Muthuswami Udaiyar on the north-eastern side of the mountain, he began composing the verses later known as Svanubhava Stotra Pamalai (Garland of Hymns of Self-Experience), 117 hymns all addressed to Lord Arunachala:

O Arunachala! Your devotees, recognising you as the infinite reality who is grace embodied in the form of fire, beyond the reach of Brahma and Vishnu, sang in praise of your greatness. I, who have in a miraculous way found you and adopted you as my Guru...

His verses are uncompromisingly advaitic in language and philosophy. He regarded Arunachala as simultaneously pure awareness, the energy that created the manifest world, and the substance out of which the world was created. Believing the best form of devotion was to abide in thought-free silence, he directed his efforts toward cultivating inner silence.

Lord Arunachaleswara himself sometimes manifested as a tiger to guard Isanya Desikar during deep meditation. A British District Collector named Ayton became one of his most devoted followers, addressing him as 'Tata' (grandfather) and receiving miraculous assistance on multiple occasions.

Isanya Desikar Samadhi, Tiruvannamalai

In 1829, foreseeing his death, Isanya Desikar told his oldest disciple cryptically, 'Lord Nataraja is going to the thousand-pillared mantapam. We too should go there.' He sat in meditation facing north and, when asked by Muthuswami Udaiyar about the fate of his devotees, replied 'Has not your family already ripened as a bunch?' — and promptly abandoned his body.

Thanks to The Mountain Path
notes: No hero image. Subtitle is dates line.
